ach_question: What is the best-supported explanation, on the evidence contained in this report, for the partial loss of engine power on the accident flight?

hypotheses.label: Fuel exhaustion
hypotheses.claim: The engine lost power because the airplane ran out of usable fuel, owing to incorrect fuel planning by the pilot.

hypotheses.label: Carburetor icing
hypotheses.claim: The engine lost power because carburetor ice formed at glide power in conditions conducive to serious icing, restricting induction airflow.

hypotheses.label: Throttle/mechanical malfunction
hypotheses.claim: The engine stopped responding because of a throttle-control or other mechanical malfunction in the engine/induction system.

evidence_rows.label: Empty tanks, no leak
evidence_rows.text_anchor: no evidence of fuel in the tanks, but fuel did not appear to have leaked from the tanks
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Physical post-accident finding of empty tanks with no leak is the strongest direct support for h1 (fuel exhaustion); it is the pivotal evidence row. If the tanks were dry and fuel did not leak out during the crash, the fuel was consumed in flight.

evidence_rows.label: Pilot expected 10+ gallons aboard
evidence_rows.text_anchor: the airplane should have contained at least 10 gallons of fuel
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Cuts against h1 on its face (fuel should have remained), but the report reframes it as evidence the pilot's fuel calculations were wrong, which supports h1's contributing-factor of improper fuel planning. Ambiguous/double-edged row.

evidence_rows.label: Conditions conducive to serious icing
evidence_rows.text_anchor: the conditions in which the airplane was operating were conducive to the formation of serious icing at glide power
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Affirmative environmental support for h2 (carburetor icing): the FAA icing probability chart placed the flight in the serious-icing-at-glide-power band.

evidence_rows.label: Pilot applied carburetor heat
evidence_rows.text_anchor: the pilot stated that he applied carburetor heat and enrichened the mixture when he began descending
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: The principal evidence against h2 (carburetor icing): carb heat is the standard mitigation, so if applied it weakens the icing explanation. Removing e4 strengthens h2.

evidence_rows.label: Gradual, throttle-unresponsive power loss
evidence_rows.text_anchor: a sudden loss of engine power (stop or change in rpm) did not occur
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Pattern evidence. A gradual fade with no sudden rpm change is consistent with progressive fuel starvation/exhaustion (h1) or ice accretion (h2), and the 'stopped responding to throttle inputs' phrasing is also superficially consistent with h3; weakly diagnostic, points away from an abrupt single mechanical seizure.

evidence_rows.label: Throttle cable travel undocumentable
evidence_rows.text_anchor: damage in the engine area that precluded documentation of the throttle cable's full range of travel
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Keeps h3 alive: investigators could not fully rule out a throttle-linkage problem because impact damage prevented checking the cable's full travel. Absence-of-evidence row that prevents fully eliminating h3.

evidence_rows.label: No restriction / no preimpact anomalies
evidence_rows.text_anchor: The cabling remained properly attached to the carburetor, and no restriction to the throttle arm was noted. No preimpact anomalies were found
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Cuts strongly against h3 (mechanical): everything that could be examined on the throttle and engine was normal, leaving only the undocumentable cable travel as a gap.

expected_read.rationale: On the full evidence set, h1 (fuel exhaustion) is best supported: the tanks were dry post-accident with no fuel leak (e1), the gradual throttle-unresponsive fade fits progressive fuel starvation (e5), and the pilot's expectation of 10+ gallons is reframed as faulty fuel planning (e2). h2 (carburetor icing) has genuine affirmative support from the conducive-icing conditions (e3) but is knocked down by the pilot applying carburetor heat (e4). h3 (mechanical) survives only because impact damage prevented documenting throttle-cable travel (e6), while everything examinable was normal (e7). The report reaches the same conclusion.
expected_read.flip_note: Removing e1 (empty tanks, no leak) removes the only direct physical anchor for fuel exhaustion. Without it, the affirmative environmental support for carburetor icing (e3) — only partially offset by the carb-heat statement (e4) — plausibly makes h2 the best-supported hypothesis, while h1 would rest only on the speculative 'fuel calculations could have been incorrect.' A real ACH/sensitivity analysis should report that dropping e1 can change the winner from h1 to h2; an engine that reports zero sensitivity here is broken.
